name: Bounce the price back — let the buyer name a number first
one_line: Never volunteer a discount; answer 'what's your lowest?' with an open question that makes the buyer reveal their budget.
why_it_works: The buyer's first number is usually above your floor; disclosing first gives away leverage and risks underselling. Probing buys information, which is leverage.
when_to_use: Any 'best price?' / 'lowest you'll go?' / 'what's it worth to you?' opener before a number is on the table.
when_not_to_use: When you want a fast sale at a fixed number and the buyer is an informed pro; trivial items where the dance costs more than the gap.
marketplace_surface: First DM reply on Depop/Vinted/Posh; eBay Best-Offer where you can wait for the buyer to offer.
example_buyer_message: What's the lowest you'll go?
recommended_reply: I'm open to a fair offer — what were you hoping to spend?

name: Calibrated / problem-solving question (deflect a demand into their problem)
one_line: Answer a lowball or demand with a deferential open 'How/What' question that makes the buyer solve your constraint instead of you saying no.
why_it_works: Creates forced empathy and the illusion of control; the buyer talks themselves up or withdraws the demand without a flat rejection that triggers a fight. Merges Voss 'How am I supposed to do that?', Ury open problem-solving question, GTY 'What's your theory?', and Shell 'probe first'.
when_to_use: Direct lowballs; 'free shipping or no deal'; 'knock off $X'; any rigid demand.
when_not_to_use: Aggressive tone turns it into an accusation; against analyst/data buyers pair it with a fact; on a clean acceptable offer just accept.
marketplace_surface: The #1 default reply to a lowball Offer on eBay/Posh/Depop; counter to 'free shipping or no deal'.
example_buyer_message: I'll give you $20.
recommended_reply: I appreciate the offer — how am I supposed to do $20 when these sell for $45? What were you hoping to spend?

name: Justify the price with sold comps (objective criteria / normative leverage)
one_line: Defend your number with an external standard — recent sold listings, condition norms, retail-minus-depreciation — reasoning first, figure second.
why_it_works: An agreement grounded in a standard is harder to attack and depersonalizes the price (conceding to the standard isn't conceding to you). People defer to standards they already accept (consistency principle). Merges GTY objective criteria, Shell normative leverage, 3-D 'because', Ury 'what makes that fair'.
when_to_use: Any price challenge, lowball, or 'competitor cheaper'; defending a firm price.
when_not_to_use: When no real comp exists (never fabricate) — pivot to interests/options; when you're genuinely above market the standard cuts against you.
marketplace_surface: Screenshotting eBay Sold range or comparable Depop/Posh sold listings in chat; one-line price justification in the description.
example_buyer_message: $45 is too much, nobody pays that.
recommended_reply: Fair to push on price — the last three of these sold for $48-$55 in this condition (happy to screenshot), so $45 is already under that. If you've seen one cheaper, send the link and I'll take a real look.

name: Decode the interest behind the position
one_line: A buyer's number ('$20') hides a real interest — budget cap, value-doubt, occasion/timing, comparison; find it and you can satisfy it without dropping price.
why_it_works: For every interest there are several positions that satisfy it; reconciling interests (not splitting positions) produces wiser deals and reveals cheap non-price fixes. Merges GTY interests, Shell 'why is the other party sitting there', 3-D 'probe behind the position', Ury reframe-to-interest.
when_to_use: Every lowball or stall before you counter; a saved/liked item that won't convert.
when_not_to_use: When the interest is obvious and met; interrogating a fast clean sale.
marketplace_surface: One interest-probing question in chat before any counter on eBay/Posh Offer.
example_buyer_message: Would you take $20? (listed $45)
recommended_reply: Happy to work with you — what were you hoping to spend, and is this for something specific? Helps me see what I can do beyond the number.

name: Hold price, add value — trade non-monetary terms (dovetail differences)
one_line: When stuck on 'how much?', keep the price and trade things cheap to you but valuable to them: free/absorbed shipping, a throw-in, a held item, faster dispatch.
why_it_works: A price cut costs you dollar-for-dollar; an option can be near-free to you and high-value to the buyer. Differences in what each side values are what make win-win deals. Merges Voss pivot-to-nonmonetary, GTY invent options/dovetail, Shell integrative trade, 3-D dovetail/move-northeast.
when_to_use: Gap between your floor and their ceiling; bundle interest; buyers who want 'a deal'.
when_not_to_use: Single cheap item where complexity annoys; buyer only wants the one item cheaper and nothing else has value to them.
marketplace_surface: Depop/Vinted shipping absorption; Posh bundle; 'I'll cover shipping instead of dropping the price'.
example_buyer_message: Can you do $25 instead of $30?
recommended_reply: I'd rather keep it at $30 since it's priced fairly — but I'll cover shipping, which works out about the same for you. Deal?

name: Bundle to grow the pie (move northeast)
one_line: Turn a one-item haggle into a larger sale: pair the wanted item with related stock at a small blended discount and combined shipping.
why_it_works: A bigger pie means each side's share can be bigger; bundling moves more inventory at a better blended margin and gives the buyer a face-saving way to a lower per-item price. Merges GTY multi-item options, Shell logrolling, 3-D move-northeast, Cialdini cooperation framing.
when_to_use: Buyer is haggling one item and you have related stock; closet clear-outs; buyer has liked multiple items.
when_not_to_use: Buyer wants exactly one specific item with no use for anything else.
marketplace_surface: Core Depop/Poshmark/Vinted bundle mechanics; eBay 'send offer to likers'.
example_buyer_message: Would you take $18 on the green tee?
recommended_reply: You've also liked the blue one — take both for $32 instead of $40 and I'll cover shipping. More for you, one package for me.

name: Shrinking-increment concession ladder, never for free
one_line: Concede in steps that each get smaller, slow visibly near your floor, end on an odd number, and attach a condition or reason to every move.
why_it_works: Decreasing increments signal you're near your limit; reciprocity makes the buyer counter; free or escalating concessions teach them to keep pushing. Merges Voss Ackerman ladder, Shell shrinking ladder + If/then, 3-D converging concessions, Cialdini rejection-then-retreat.
when_to_use: Multi-message haggles with a committed buyer; higher-value items worth the back-and-forth.
when_not_to_use: Low-value quick flips; first lowball you should rule out instead; when a strong BATNA means you shouldn't move at all.
marketplace_surface: eBay Best-Offer / Posh counter sequencing (e.g. $50 -> $44 -> $41 -> $40 firm).
recommended_reply: I came down to $44 already — I can do $43 if we close now, and that's really as far as I can go on this one.
conflict_notes: See PRIN-008: Voss 'don't split the difference' vs the reciprocal concession dance. Reconciled by conceding on a shrinking ladder to your real floor rather than reflexively meeting at the midpoint.

name: Don't reflexively split the difference — no deal beats a bad deal
one_line: Refuse the automatic 'meet me halfway'; counter with a small odd move or a term, anchored to your floor, and be ready to relist.
why_it_works: The midpoint of two arbitrary numbers is itself arbitrary; reciprocity drags outcomes to the midpoint, so symmetric moves give value away. Readiness to walk removes neediness, which is leverage. Merges Voss don't-compromise, 3-D midpoint-rule warning, Shell goal-not-floor anchoring.
when_to_use: Buyer pushes 'just split it'; thin-margin items; serial discounters.
when_not_to_use: When the item is aging, you need cash/space, and 'halfway' still clears your floor — know your floor first.
marketplace_surface: Countering 'let's just do $35' on any platform; eBay Best-Offer auto-decline below floor.
example_buyer_message: You're at $40, I'm at $30, let's just do $35.
recommended_reply: I get the instinct to split it, but $35 doesn't quite work for me. I can do $38 today, otherwise no worries — I'm happy to hold out.
conflict_notes: DIRECT CONFLICT with GTY (PRIN-003), which says splitting between two figures each backed by a real standard is fair. Reconciliation: never split two arbitrary numbers (Voss), but a split that lands inside the comp-supported fair range is acceptable; gate the midpoint behind your floor and a standard.

name: Anchor on your goal, not your floor (high-but-defensible list price)
one_line: Set the listing and counters at the top of the defensible fair range and treat that as your reference point; keep your walk-away floor out of focus.
why_it_works: People who expect more generally get more; anchoring on the floor makes you relax and stop striving the moment an offer clears it, and buyers sense that relaxation. The first number sets the zone. Merges Shell goals-vs-bottom-line + anchor effect, 3-D anchoring, Cialdini contrast.
when_to_use: Setting list price; making the first counter; whenever you have leverage and a comp to cite.
when_not_to_use: When you lack leverage and the buyer knows it; aging unsold item; relationship outweighs stakes (open closer to fair); never an anchor that fails the giggle test.
marketplace_surface: List near the top of the eBay Sold range rather than the average; counter near list, not the middle.
example_buyer_message: What's the lowest you'll go?
recommended_reply: Based on what these have sold for recently ($48-$55), $50 is already a good deal — that's where I'm at. Happy to bundle if you want more value.
conflict_notes: Tension with GTY (which prefers a BATNA-based reservation over an anchor) — reconciled: anchor high to claim value, but the anchor must be comp-defensible (PRIN-003) and the accept/decline decision is governed by BATNA (PRIN-010).

name: Know your BATNA — relist/hold is your walk-away (and your trip wire)
one_line: Decide before negotiating what you'll do if it doesn't sell (relist, cross-list, hold, keep) and judge every offer against that, with a 'reconsider below $X' trip wire instead of a rigid bottom line.
why_it_works: A BATNA is a smarter measure than a guessed bottom line and tells you exactly when to walk; the better your BATNA the greater your power. A trip wire keeps flexibility while blocking a regret-accept in the heat of the moment. Merges GTY BATNA + trip wire, Ury prepare-the-prize, Shell leverage, 3-D no-deal options.
when_to_use: Setting accept/counter/decline thresholds; any 'final offer' moment; deciding whether to take a sitting offer.
when_not_to_use: As a static number — recompute as saves/watchers/days-listed change; an aging zero-interest listing has a weaker BATNA, so be more flexible.
marketplace_surface: eBay Best-Offer auto-decline threshold; copilot 'this is under your reconsider line — counter, hold, or accept?' prompt.
example_buyer_message: $30 take it or leave it.
recommended_reply: I appreciate the straight offer. At $30 I'd genuinely rather relist — it's getting steady interest at $45. I could meet you at $40 today, though, and we're done. Deal?

name: Shape the buyer's no-deal option with truthful scarcity
one_line: Make the buyer aware — only when true — that their alternative is worse: last one, real saves/watchers, not restocking, price rising after the sale.
why_it_works: Outcomes improve when the buyer sees their walk-away as bad and getting worse; scarcity raises perceived value and loss aversion pushes the Act-Now button. Merges GTY shape-their-BATNA, Shell improve-leverage, 3-D worsen-no-deal, Cialdini scarcity/loss-framing, Voss loss-aversion anchor.
when_to_use: Hesitating buyer at close when genuine competing interest or a real constraint exists.
when_not_to_use: GUARDRAIL: never fabricate other buyers, fake countdowns, or a relist date that isn't real. Mass-available items.
marketplace_surface: '9 people have this saved'; 'last one in this size, not restocking'; genuine ship cutoff; only on real like-count / single-quantity data.
example_buyer_message: Let me think about it / maybe next payday.
recommended_reply: No pressure at all! Just so you're not disappointed — it's the only one I have and a couple of people have it saved, so I can't promise it'll still be here. Here whenever you're ready.

name: Mirror their last words to make them elaborate
one_line: Repeat the critical last one-to-three words of the buyer's message with an upward inflection, then go silent.
why_it_works: Triggers the bonding instinct, makes the buyer re-word and reveal budget or motive, and buys time without disagreeing.
when_to_use: Vague or probing messages; when you want them to reveal their hand before you commit to a number.
when_not_to_use: When the buyer has made a clean firm offer you want to accept; overuse in one thread reads robotic.
marketplace_surface: First reply before any number; reacting to a lowball comment under a listing.
example_buyer_message: Would you take a lot less? It's been listed a while.
recommended_reply: A lot less?

name: Label the emotion to defuse it ('It seems like...')
one_line: Name the buyer's likely feeling as a neutral statement (never starting with 'I'), then let it sit.
why_it_works: Labeling a feeling shifts brain activity from the amygdala to rational areas, defusing negatives and reinforcing positives — a shortcut to feeling understood. Merges Voss labeling and DC acknowledge-feelings.
when_to_use: Buyer is hesitant, frustrated, suspicious, or stalling; clearing emotional barriers before talking price.
when_not_to_use: On calm transactional messages (sounds patronizing); don't manufacture emotion; don't stack a question right after.
marketplace_surface: Responding to a hesitant 'hmm not sure' DM; defusing annoyance about shipping cost or a slow reply.
example_buyer_message: Idk, $40 feels like a lot for something secondhand.
recommended_reply: It sounds like you want to be sure you're getting real value before you commit — totally fair.

name: Accusation audit — pre-empt the negative before you deliver it
one_line: Name the worst thing the buyer could think about your firm price or flaw disclosure, and say it first to take the sting out.
why_it_works: Denying a negative gives it credence; voicing it first disarms it and often triggers the buyer to insist the opposite is true.
when_to_use: Delivering a firm price, declining a bundle, or disclosing a small defect before they find it.
when_not_to_use: When there's no real objection to pre-empt (manufacturing fake negatives reads weird); don't over-apologize into weakness.
marketplace_surface: Opening a 'sorry, firm price' message; declining a lowball; flaw disclosure in chat.
example_buyer_message: Can you do $15 instead of $30?
recommended_reply: You're probably going to think I'm being stubborn — I get it. The truth is I've already priced this below similar ones, so I can't go to $15. But I'd love to find a number that works.

name: Use a 'No'-oriented question to re-engage a ghosting buyer
one_line: Revive a silent buyer with one short question that invites 'No' and implies you'll move on ('Have you given up on this one?').
why_it_works: 'No' reaffirms autonomy and lowers guard; loss aversion makes the buyer reply to correct you and reclaim control rather than be abandoned. Merges Voss 'No'-oriented re-engagement and Ury keep-the-offer-on-the-table.
when_to_use: Buyer who made an offer or asked about availability then went silent 24h+.
when_not_to_use: More than once per buyer (becomes nagging); on a buyer who already clearly declined.
marketplace_surface: The anti-ghosting nudge; a buyer sitting on a sent counteroffer about to expire.
example_buyer_message: (silence after 'is this still available?')
recommended_reply: Have you given up on this jacket? Totally fine if it's no longer right — just want to know whether to keep holding it for you.

name: Drive to 'That's right' with a summary
one_line: Paraphrase the buyer's situation plus their underlying feeling so the only response is 'That's right' (not the dismissive 'you're right').
why_it_works: 'That's right' is genuine buy-in; 'you're right' means they want you to stop talking and nothing will change. Confirms true alignment before the close.
when_to_use: Stuck negotiations, repeat hagglers, or before a final counter; converting a chatty browser.
when_not_to_use: As a manipulation to fake agreement you haven't actually understood.
marketplace_surface: Before sending a final counter; converting a buyer who keeps asking but not buying.
example_buyer_message: I love it but I'm trying not to overspend and I've been burned by sizing online before.
recommended_reply: So it sounds like you really want the piece, you just need to feel confident the fit is right and that you're not blowing your budget on a guess.

name: Use odd, precise numbers for a final/firm price
one_line: Make your final counter end in a non-round number; round numbers read as placeholders, odd ones as a calculated real limit.
why_it_works: A precise figure signals you've reached your actual limit and is psychologically harder to negotiate off of.
when_to_use: Final counteroffer; the 'firm' price you won't move from.
when_not_to_use: Tiny-dollar items where precision looks silly.
marketplace_surface: Counter at $43 not $45; 'best I can do is $42.50 shipped'.
example_buyer_message: Meet me at $40?
recommended_reply: I can come down to $43 — that's genuinely the lowest the numbers work for me.

name: Reasoning before the number; make the close one easy 'yes'
one_line: Lead with the standard/reason, then the figure as 'one fair option', shaped as a single yes/no the buyer can accept in one word.
why_it_works: Lead with a number and the buyer stops listening and starts countering; lead with reasoning and they follow you to the conclusion. A 'yesable proposition' clinches deals.
when_to_use: Sending a counter or final price; closing a long haggle.
when_not_to_use: Over-explaining a trivial price (reads defensive); burying a simple accept under a paragraph.
marketplace_surface: Counter messages on any platform; final price line.
example_buyer_message: So what's your actual best?
recommended_reply: Given the condition and that comparable ones sell for $48-55, here's a fair one: $44 shipped, and I'll toss in the dust bag. That work?

name: Go to the balcony — pause before replying to a triggering message
one_line: When a message provokes you, suspend the instinct to strike back/cave/break off; draft, re-read calm, then send.
why_it_works: The first casualty of an attack is your objectivity; reacting hooks you into the buyer's game. Text strips tone, so your worst-case reading is as likely wrong as right. Merges Ury balcony and DC hit-pause/question-attributions.
when_to_use: The instant an insult, derisive lowball, or ambiguous terse message lands and you want to fire back.
when_not_to_use: Genuinely time-sensitive logistics; don't let the pause become indefinite silence that reads as ghosting.
marketplace_surface: Copilot cool-down: on an angry seller draft or hostile inbound, suggest waiting and offer a calmer draft.
example_buyer_message: $10 take it or leave it, this isn't worth what you're asking lol
recommended_reply: Thanks for the offer! Let me take a look at where I can land on price and get back to you in a sec.

name: Step to their side — acknowledge before you counter
one_line: Let the buyer feel heard first (paraphrase the concern, validate it as reasonable) before defending price or saying no.
why_it_works: Listening may be the cheapest concession you can make; a buyer who feels heard becomes less reactive and far more willing to hear your terms. Merges Ury step-to-their-side, DC acknowledge-feelings, Voss tactical empathy.
when_to_use: Buyer is venting, frustrated, or has piled several complaints into one message.
when_not_to_use: On a clean offer you'll accept (just close); don't manufacture a 'tell me more' detour.
marketplace_surface: Opening line of any complaint/objection reply across platforms.
example_buyer_message: This is overpriced. Secondhand stuff shouldn't cost this much and the photos are dark.
recommended_reply: I hear you — you want to be sure it's worth it and that you can actually see what you're getting. Totally reasonable. Let me add brighter photos and walk you through the price.

name: Say 'Yes... and', never 'but'; speak in 'I' not 'you'
one_line: Express disagreement as an addition ('yes, and...') rather than a contradiction, and state your view as your perception ('the way I see it') not a verdict on the buyer.
why_it_works: 'But' signals 'you're wrong' and makes the buyer stop listening; 'and' lets your position coexist with theirs. 'You are X' provokes 'says who?'; 'I see/feel' can't be argued with. Merges Ury yes-and/I-statements and DC speak-for-yourself + And-Stance.
when_to_use: Whenever you must disagree on price/condition/terms while keeping the buyer engaged.
when_not_to_use: Don't disguise an accusation as an I-statement; don't water a verifiable fact down into a 'feeling'.
marketplace_surface: Reply-generator formatting rule: strip 'but' -> 'and'; convert 'you're wrong about the price' -> 'the way I see it, the price reflects...'.
example_buyer_message: Your price is too high for the quality.
recommended_reply: Yes, it's at the higher end — and that's because it's barely worn and authenticated. The way I see it, that's where the value is.

name: Build a golden bridge — give a face-saving way to climb down
one_line: Don't push a stuck buyer toward your price; frame their move as a win (bundle deal, 'prices just dropped', new-buyer deal) so retreating from their lowball looks like an advance.
why_it_works: Resistance often comes from fear of losing face after a public/committed stand; pushing widens the gap, a bridge reframes the retreat as a victory. Merges Ury golden-bridge/save-face and Cialdini liking/labeled-concession.
when_to_use: Buyer is close but stuck after holding a hard line (e.g. commented '$25 max') and needs a way up.
when_not_to_use: When no real interest of theirs exists to meet and a bridge would just lose you money; GUARDRAIL: the cover must be true, not a fabricated sale.
marketplace_surface: Posh/Depop bundles, eBay 'send offer to likers', Vinted bundle discount as the cover for a price move.
example_buyer_message: I told you $25 is my max.
recommended_reply: Tell you what — I'm running a bundle deal this week, so if you add the matching item I can do the pair for $40, which gets this one near your number. Want me to set that up?

name: Warn with your BATNA, don't threaten; keep the offer on the table
one_line: State the consequence of no-deal as a neutral fact ('these tend to sell fast, I'll probably keep it listed') while leaving your best offer visible, never as an angry 'I'll sell to someone else'.
why_it_works: A warning is objective and respectful; a threat triggers ego and fighting back. The contrast between 'no deal' and an attractive standing offer reduces resistance more than punishment. Merges Ury warn-not-threaten + keep-sharpening-the-choice.
when_to_use: Final stage where the buyer is stalling or testing whether you'll cave and your real BATNA is to relist.
when_not_to_use: As a first move (reads aggressive); when relisting genuinely isn't a good alternative; never bluff a BATNA you don't have.
marketplace_surface: eBay relist, 'X people have this liked', Depop/Posh relist-and-share; a low-pressure re-engagement that restates the standing offer.
example_buyer_message: I'm not going a cent over $25. Final.
recommended_reply: All good! I'll keep it at $38 for now — a couple people have it liked so it may sell soon, but if you change your mind it's yours at $38. No worries either way.

name: Be generous at the end — close for mutual satisfaction
one_line: When closing after a haggle, concede a tiny final crumb (free shipping, fast dispatch, a sticker) and let the buyer feel they won the last round.
why_it_works: An imposed/crushed buyer may renege, leave bad feedback, or never return; a satisfied one completes the sale, reviews well, and comes back. Merges Ury aim-for-satisfaction, GTY generous-final-gesture, Cialdini reciprocity.
when_to_use: Closing any deal, especially after a tense haggle.
when_not_to_use: Don't confuse generosity with being exploited — give the last small crumb, not your whole margin; don't re-drop price after saying 'final'.
marketplace_surface: Closing-message template; the small add-on rather than another price drop.
example_buyer_message: Ok I'll take it at $38. Can you ship today?
recommended_reply: Done — $38 it is, and yes I'll get it out today. I think you got a great deal on this one. I'll send tracking once it's on its way!

name: Close for commitment, not just agreement (get it in writing, on-platform)
one_line: Turn 'I'll take it' into an action that makes backing out costly: confirm the terms in the thread, then send the offer/invoice immediately and keep it on-platform.
why_it_works: A 'yes' isn't a deal until the buyer has something to lose if they renege; written/active commitments bind harder than verbal ones and cut ghosting. Merges Shell commitment-not-agreement, Cialdini get-it-in-writing, Voss Rule-of-Three.
when_to_use: The moment a buyer agrees to a price.
when_not_to_use: Don't demand heavy commitment devices on a tiny trusted sale (a quick checkout is enough); don't shame a hesitant buyer into it.
marketplace_surface: Send the eBay Best-Offer/invoice or Depop/Posh payment link right after agreement; restate price + shipping in chat.
example_buyer_message: Ok yeah let's do $45.
recommended_reply: Perfect — confirming $45 plus $5 shipping, total $50. I've just sent the offer; accept it whenever you're ready and I'll pack and ship today.

name: Detect the buyer's bargaining style and match your move
one_line: Read the opener for style (competing / collaborating / compromising / accommodating / avoiding) and pick a matched approach instead of one-size-fits-all.
why_it_works: The right move against a competitive flipper (firm + leverage) is wrong against an accommodating sentimental buyer (relationship + generosity). Reading style first prevents misfiring. Merges Shell five styles, 3-D claimer-vs-creator, Cialdini buyer personas.
when_to_use: Every thread at first contact — the opener is the richest style signal.
when_not_to_use: Don't over-classify on one ambiguous line; hold the label loosely and update as cues arrive.
marketplace_surface: Drives which reply template surfaces on the first DM / Best-Offer.
example_buyer_message: Love this! Would you maybe consider a small discount? Totally fine if not :)
recommended_reply: Thank you, that's so kind! I can do a little something since you clearly love it — $X works and I'll pack it really carefully for you.

name: Match firmness to relationship vs. stakes (situational matrix)
one_line: Dial firmness by whether you'll deal with this buyer again and how high the stakes are: one-off stranger -> firm + anchor; repeat/local -> accommodate; both high -> collaborate.
why_it_works: Different situations reward different styles; most resale is low-stakes coordination where smoothness wins, but a one-off high-value sale rewards leverage and a repeat buyer rewards generosity.
when_to_use: Choosing tone/firmness at the start of a thread; when a repeat or local buyer appears.
when_not_to_use: Don't over-invest relationship effort in a one-off cheap sale to a stranger.
marketplace_surface: Copilot detects repeat buyers/followers/locals vs. one-time strangers, and item value/sentiment, then dials firmness.
example_buyer_message: Hey, I bought from you before — any chance of a deal on this one too?
recommended_reply: Of course, repeat buyers are my favourite! I'll knock a bit off for you — $X and I'll get it shipped today. Thanks for coming back.

name: Use the norm of reciprocity — take turns
one_line: After you give a little (a small concession or disclosure), pause and let the buyer reciprocate before giving again; a well-placed small give triggers their obligation to move.
why_it_works: People feel obliged to return concessions and disclosures; 'negotiation is a dance and reciprocity is its rhythm'. Resist the trap of small-gives-for-big-asks. Merges Shell reciprocity, Cialdini reciprocation.
when_to_use: Stalled threads; after you've moved and they've gone quiet; to break a disclosure deadlock.
when_not_to_use: When the buyer is exploiting it (one-sided pattern) — hold; don't name the debt out loud (it works best unspoken).
marketplace_surface: 'I came down $5, can you come up $5?'; a small first concession to restart a quiet thread.
example_buyer_message: Hmm, can you do a bit better?
recommended_reply: I'll meet you partway — I'll do $46 if you can come up to that. Fair trade?

name: Surface genuine social proof to lower buyer risk
one_line: Cite real demand evidence — likes/saves, sold counts, review count and rating, shop tenure — to answer 'is this any good / are you legit?'.
why_it_works: Under uncertainty buyers judge quality by what others are doing; quantified real demand removes risk without you arguing the item is good, strongest from similar buyers. GUARDRAIL: only ever true numbers.
when_to_use: Listing has real traction; nervous/first-time buyer; 'is this legit?'.
when_not_to_use: No real demand yet — never invent counts, saves, or similar-buyer personas.
marketplace_surface: Depop/Posh likes, 'X saved', 'sold 3 already', star rating + review count, 'from a 200+ sale shop'.
example_buyer_message: Is this actually any good? bit nervous buying secondhand.
recommended_reply: Totally fair! For what it's worth, 14 people have this saved and I've sold two of the same style this month — both buyers left 5 stars. Happy to send extra photos too.

name: Answer trust/authenticity with genuine proof (authority)
one_line: Meet authenticity and condition doubts with real evidence — receipts, serial/date codes, stitching close-ups, demonstrated specifics — not bare reassurance.
why_it_works: Buyers defer to credible authority and proof; for a stranger online, demonstrated knowledge directly answers the trust objection. GUARDRAIL: never claim expertise/authentication you don't have.
when_to_use: Buyer questions authenticity, condition, or your competence and you have the proof.
when_not_to_use: Claiming credentials you lack; implying authentication you can't back.
marketplace_surface: Receipt photo, batch/serial codes, date code, detail close-ups; verified badge only if real.
example_buyer_message: How do I know this bag is real?
recommended_reply: Great question — I've added photos of the date code (inside the side pocket) and the original receipt. The hardware's engraved, not stamped, which is one of the real-vs-fake tells. Happy to photograph any detail you want.

name: Build liking with genuine rapport, similarity, and sincere compliments
one_line: Match the buyer's tone, surface real shared taste, and acknowledge a fair offer or good eye honestly — be a human, not a script.
why_it_works: We comply more with people we like and who are similar to us; cheap to build, powerful in effect, and it smooths haggling. GUARDRAIL: genuine only — fake similarity/empty flattery backfires.
when_to_use: Throughout chat; with enthusiast buyers; when the buyer shows good taste or makes a fair offer.
when_not_to_use: Manufacturing fake similarity or insincere praise as a lever.
marketplace_surface: Match emoji/casual vs precise tone; 'great eye, this is one of my favourites'; 'fair offer, I appreciate that'.
example_buyer_message: omg I've been hunting for this colourway forever
recommended_reply: Honestly same — this colourway is so hard to find, I almost kept it. You'll love it. Want a couple more pics in daylight?

name: Ladder small yeses toward the purchase (commitment & consistency)
one_line: Secure an easy genuine yes (answer a question, confirm a size, 'want me to hold it?') and let purchase be the natural consistent next step.
why_it_works: A small initial commitment shifts self-image and makes a later larger consistent action more likely; the like itself is the first micro-commitment.
when_to_use: Early in a chat to convert an 'is it available?' or a measurement question.
when_not_to_use: Engineering a trivial commitment purely to trap someone; escalating pressure once they hesitate.
marketplace_surface: answer-a-question -> 'want me to reserve it?' -> checkout, in Depop/Vinted DMs.
example_buyer_message: Do you have measurements?
recommended_reply: Yes! Pit-to-pit 52cm, length 70cm — sounds like it'd fit you. Want me to hold it for a bit while you check?

name: Restore the buyer's freedom to defuse reactance
one_line: State true scarcity once, then explicitly hand control back ('no pressure, it's here whenever you're ready'); avoid repeated nudges and countdowns.
why_it_works: When freedom is threatened people want the thing less and may walk; giving them their freedom often closes better than pressure. This is the safety-default tone. Merges Cialdini reactance and DC let-go-of-controlling-their-reaction.
when_to_use: Hesitant or independent-minded buyer; whenever you're tempted to push hard; 'stop messaging me, I'm thinking'.
when_not_to_use: Never replace with manufactured can't-come-back pressure.
marketplace_surface: Default copilot tone; one factual scarcity note at most, then back off.
example_buyer_message: Stop messaging me, I'm still thinking.
recommended_reply: Of course — no pressure at all, take your time. It's here whenever you're ready and I'm happy to answer anything.

name: Run a 3-D barriers audit before dropping price
one_line: When a listing stalls, diagnose which dimension is blocking — weak setup (photos/comps/audience), bad deal design (price-only, no bundle/shipping lever), or table tactics — and fix the highest broken one first.
why_it_works: A problem in one dimension usually has its fix in another; slashing price (tactics) when the real barrier is a weak listing (setup) wastes effort and leaves money on the table.
when_to_use: A listing has stalled, drawn only lowballs, or had repeated no-sales — before any price cut or relist.
when_not_to_use: First few days of a fresh well-built listing — give setup time to work.
marketplace_surface: Copilot 'why isn't this selling?' diagnostic: scores photo count, comp-anchored price, bundle eligibility, reply latency before recommending a cut.
example_buyer_message: (internal: 40 likes, 0 offers in 3 weeks)
recommended_reply: Before you drop the price: photos and price look strong, but there's no bundle offer set and shipping is buyer-paid at $8 — two things buyers hesitate on. Want to add a 'bundle 2+ and save' and bake shipping into the price instead of cutting it?

name: Route the listing to the highest-value buyer (parties + framing)
one_line: Don't accept current lowballers as fixed; ask who would value this item most and reach them via platform, tags, and framing (styled flat-lay, exact collector model, 'NWT designer').
why_it_works: The single biggest setup lever is negotiating with the party who values the item most — they offer fairly without haggling; the frame sets the price ceiling before any number. Merges 3-D map-the-parties + meta-anchor-the-frame. GUARDRAIL: framing must be honest.
when_to_use: At listing time (platform/tags/title/cover photo) and when current offers are all bargain-hunter lowballs.
when_not_to_use: Pure commodity with one obvious mass market; never inflate condition or invent provenance.
marketplace_surface: Cross-listing strategy; Depop styled flat-lays + niche tags; eBay exact-compatibility model; title/cover-photo framing.
example_buyer_message: (two lowballs at 40% within an hour of posting)
recommended_reply: These lowballs suggest you're reaching bargain-hunters, not the people who'd pay full price. Let's re-tag with the specific line name and post a styled photo — 'Reformation Linen Midi, Worn Once' anchors higher than 'black dress' and reaches buyers who offer fairly.

name: Bridge doubt with contingent terms and risk allocation
one_line: Answer 'is it real / will it fit / what if it breaks?' with terms — returns, platform authentication, tracked+insured shipping, measurements — rather than a price cut.
why_it_works: Contingent terms let optimists and skeptics both say yes by betting on their own forecast and act as a 'truth serum'; buyers pay a premium for certainty a seller can cheaply provide. Merges 3-D contingent-terms + allocate-risk.
when_to_use: Authenticity doubt, fit doubt, condition skepticism, high-ticket or fragile-item hesitation.
when_not_to_use: Low-value items where return shipping exceeds item value; platforms where you can't control return terms.
marketplace_surface: Lean on eBay Authenticity Guarantee / Posh authenticate-over-$500, offer-returns settings, double-boxed insured shipping, clear measurements.
example_buyer_message: How do I know it's not a fake for $400?
recommended_reply: Great question — at $400+ it ships through eBay's Authenticity Guarantee, so an expert inspects it before it reaches you and you're fully refunded if it's not genuine. Neither of us is taking a risk.

name: Set the spirit of the deal in the listing (prevent disputes)
one_line: State condition honestly (with flaw photos), ship time, and return policy up front so post-sale reality matches buyer expectations.
why_it_works: Deals unravel when parties hold divergent unspoken expectations; embedding them in the listing pre-empts 'item not as described' cases, returns, and bad reviews.
when_to_use: Every listing; especially flawed/used items, slow-shipping situations, made-to-order.
when_not_to_use: n/a — always worth it; keep it concise so it doesn't read defensive.
marketplace_surface: Listing-description generator that proactively states condition, flaws, ship time, and returns.
example_buyer_message: (drafting a listing for a worn item)
recommended_reply: Add to the description: 'Pre-loved — small mark on left cuff shown in photo 4. Ships within 2 business days from a smoke-free home. Bundle to save on shipping.' Setting this up front prevents surprises and bad reviews.

name: Time the listing and step price down on a cadence
one_line: List/refresh at high-traffic times (evenings, weekends, paydays, season-start), hold price through an initial window, then relist and step down in small scheduled increments instead of a panic cut.
why_it_works: Differences in patience create gains; dropping price too fast leaves money on the table, and a fresh relist resets feed position. Timing is a setup lever, not a reaction.
when_to_use: Deciding when to list, when to relist/refresh, and how fast to lower price.
when_not_to_use: Genuinely time-sensitive inventory (perishable trend, dated event item).
marketplace_surface: Depop/Vinted feed rewards fresh listings; copilot recommends relist timing and a measured markdown ladder.
example_buyer_message: (item unsold 10 days, seller wants to slash 30%)
recommended_reply: Hold off on a 30% cut — it's coming up on payday weekend. Let's relist to refresh your feed position and, if it's still here next week, step down 10% rather than 30%.

name: Treat 'is this still available?' as a low-signal buying cue
one_line: Answer warmly, name yourself, and add one forward-motion step (a small-yes or true scarcity) instead of a dead 'yes it's available'.
why_it_works: It's a weak intent signal but still an opening; a low-friction yes plus gentle motion converts the check-in without over-negotiating a ready buyer. Merges NSD/GTY/Cialdini/3-D handling of the availability ping.
when_to_use: Any 'is this still available?' / 'still got this?' message.
when_not_to_use: Don't launch a full negotiation or pressure sequence off a one-line ping.
marketplace_surface: Depop/Vinted/Posh availability DMs.
example_buyer_message: Is this still available?
recommended_reply: Yes it is! I'm Sam — it's the last one, want me to hold it for you or were you looking to grab it now?

name: Handle bargaining bullies on leverage, route threats to safety
one_line: When a buyer uses rudeness, ultimatums, or review-extortion as a tactic, drop relationship moves, hold firm on leverage, don't reward it, and route real threats to a calm decline + platform report.
why_it_works: Bullies respect only strength; accommodation invites more. A small concession reads as weakness; raising their cost of persisting and being comfortable with no-deal is the answer. Merges Shell bargaining-bullies, DC name-the-dynamic/clarify-consequences, Voss don't-counter-punch.
when_to_use: Rude/aggressive buyers, serial nibblers, 'discount or I leave a bad review'.
when_not_to_use: A merely blunt-but-reasonable competer (use standards/leverage normally); never match abuse or retaliate.
marketplace_surface: Declining review-extortion; copilot routes harassment/threats to a documented decline + report path.
example_buyer_message: Give me 40% off or I'll leave you a one-star review.
recommended_reply: I'm sorry you feel that way, but I price fairly and reviews should reflect the actual item and service. I can't do 40% off. If you'd still like it at the listed price I'm happy to help — otherwise no worries. (Documenting the thread in case the platform needs it.)

name: Disentangle intent from impact when accused of scamming
one_line: When a buyer says 'you scammed me / you knew it was fake', acknowledge the harm and feeling first, set the intent-accusation aside, and invite evidence — don't protest your innocence before they feel heard.
why_it_works: Buyers attribute intent from impact ('I feel cheated, therefore you cheated me'); protesting 'I'd never scam you!' first changes the topic from their hurt to your character and makes them angrier.
when_to_use: trust-authenticity and rude-aggressive disputes; 'this is a scam', 'you sent me a fake', 'you did this on purpose'.
when_not_to_use: When actual fraud evidence is needed (still acknowledge first, then provide facts); don't over-apologize into implied guilt you don't have.
marketplace_surface: Defusing 'scammer' accusations before a dispute/case opens; reduces retaliatory negative reviews.
example_buyer_message: You KNEW this bag was fake when you sold it to me. Scammer.
recommended_reply: I hear that you think this isn't authentic and you feel cheated — that would upset anyone and I take it seriously. I didn't sell it believing it was anything but genuine, and I want to get to the bottom of it. Can you send photos of the date code? If it's not right, I'll make it right.

name: Own your real contribution early when something went wrong
one_line: When you genuinely share fault (vague listing, slow ship, missed flaw), own it specifically and up front, then offer fair options — don't wait to be cornered.
why_it_works: Acknowledging your part early signals you've left the blame game, lowers defensiveness, shrinks the buyer's demand, and reads as confident not weak. Merges DC take-responsibility + blame-to-contribution.
when_to_use: Late shipping, unlisted defect, misleading photo, wrong item sent.
when_not_to_use: When you did nothing wrong (don't over-apologize into liability); when fraud is alleged, be precise about what you own.
marketplace_surface: Opening a complaint reply by owning the specific miss, which de-escalates and often shrinks the demand.
example_buyer_message: There's a stain on the inside you didn't mention!!
recommended_reply: You're right and that's on me — I missed that stain in the listing and should have flagged it. I'm sorry. I'd like to make it fair: a partial refund to keep it, or a full refund on return — your call.

name: Resolve disputes with two options + a fair standard
one_line: After feelings are acknowledged, offer two concrete options that protect your core interest (full refund on return OR keep-it partial) and anchor on a fair standard (platform policy, listed measurements).
why_it_works: Two options give the buyer agency; a fair standard lets either side back down without losing face, turning a willpower contest into a solvable problem. Merges DC problem-solve-with-options and GTY/Shell standards.
when_to_use: Landing a refund/return/partial/replacement after acknowledgment.
when_not_to_use: Before feelings are acknowledged (premature problem-solving short-circuits resolution).
marketplace_surface: Closing a return negotiation on Posh/eBay/Vinted citing buyer-protection terms as the neutral standard.
example_buyer_message: I want a refund but I don't want to pay to ship it back.
recommended_reply: Totally fair to not want shipping hassle. Two options: I send a prepaid return label and refund you in full once it's back, or you keep it and I refund 25% now — in line with how the platform handles a minor not-as-described. Which works better?

name: Refuse off-platform payment on principle, not suspicion (safety override)
one_line: Decline off-platform pay / 'pay outside the app' warmly using an 'I feel safer keeping it on the app, it protects us both' frame — never negotiate a scam signal.
why_it_works: On-platform keeps buyer/seller protection and gives both sides 'something to lose'; framing it as mutual protection (not 'I don't trust you') keeps the channel open while holding the line. This is a hard safety stop, not a negotiation lever. Merges GTY trust-is-a-separate-issue, DC I-feel, all-books safety overlay.
when_to_use: 'Venmo/PayPal F&F outside the app', 'pay this link', 'ship first and I'll pay', code-sharing, overpayment/refund setups.
when_not_to_use: Never relax it; balcony/bridge logic does NOT apply to scam signals — hard-stop and, if pressed, disengage/report.
marketplace_surface: Safety layer across all platforms; decline + keep deal on-platform.
source_books: GTY; GPN; BFA; 3DN; INF; DC; NSD
example_buyer_message: Just send it to my address and I'll Venmo you outside the app, trust me.
recommended_reply: I appreciate you wanting to make it easy! I feel more comfortable keeping it on the app though — it protects us both if anything goes sideways. Happy to go ahead at the agreed price right here whenever you're ready.
conflict_notes: Synthesized/INFERRED across all books: every profile flags off-platform as a safety overlay the books themselves do not cover. The negotiation framing (decline-on-principle, 'I feel') is sourced; the scam taxonomy (overpayment, fake screenshots, chargebacks) is marketplace data, not from the books.

name: Coach the seller around their own style's blind spot
one_line: Counter the user's bargaining-style weakness: nudge accommodators/compromisers to hold a round and ask before caving; remind competers to warm up with repeat buyers; prompt avoiders to make the ask.
why_it_works: Each style has a failure mode (accommodators get exploited, compromisers concede too fast, competers bruise relationships and miss non-price value, avoiders never ask); coaching to the gap is where the copilot adds the most seller-side value. Merges Shell coach-the-style and DC shifter-vs-absorber.
when_to_use: Continuously as a coaching layer; especially when the user is about to under-ask or over-cave.
when_not_to_use: Don't override a deliberate, situation-appropriate choice (an accommodator gifting a regular is fine).
marketplace_surface: A draft-reply check that flags 'this concession is bigger than needed', 'you haven't asked a single question', or 'you're about to accept below your goal'.
example_buyer_message: (user about to send 'ok fine $25 is fine' — listed $40, first offer was $20)
recommended_reply: Before you send that — you're going from $40 to $25 on their first lowball, which signals lots of room. Try: 'I can do $35 if you grab it today.' You can always come down again.
